1: The superscription repeats the al-tašḥēt miktam of David formula from Ps 57. The opening address is contested: "Do you indeed decree what is right, you ʾĕlōhîm?" The plural ʾĕlōhîm could refer to human judges (as in Exod 21:6; 22:8–9, where judges are called ʾĕlōhîm) or to divine council members — supernatural beings charged with governing the nations (Deut 32:8 LXX; Ps 82:1–6; Dan 10:13). If the divine council interpretation is correct, the Psalm is a prophetic address to heavenly overseers who have allowed injustice to flourish on earth — a tradition prominent in Psalm 82 (where God judges the divine council for failing to uphold justice: "How long will you judge unjustly?"). Either way, the poem is a prophetic denunciation of unjust rulers, human or divine.
2: "No — in your heart you devise wrongs; your hands administer violence throughout the land" — the interior origin of injustice: bĕlēb ʿawlōt tipʿāllûn (in your heart you work wickednesses). The corruption of justice begins not in ignorance but in the heart — a deliberate choice to use authority for violence. "Throughout the land (bāʾāreṣ)" — the scope is comprehensive; this is not localized corruption but systemic. In covenant theology, rulers were to be agents of divine justice; rulers who devise evil from the heart have become anti-covenant figures, using their office as a weapon against the very people they were appointed to protect (Deut 17:14–20; 1 Sam 8:10–18).
3: "The wicked are alienated from the womb; from their very birth they go astray, speaking lies" — zōrû rĕšāʿîm mēraḥem (the wicked are estranged from the womb). This verse asserts the deep-rootedness of wickedness in those who practice it — not a surface habit but a condition present from birth. This is a wisdom observation about observed human nature, not a systematic statement about infant guilt. The verse has a parallel in Ps 51:5 ("I was sinful at birth, sinful from the time my mother conceived me") — both acknowledge that human moral formation begins before conscious choice. "Speaking lies" (dōbĕrê šāqer) identifies the characteristic expression of this inborn tendency: falsehood as the natural language of the estranged heart.
4: "Their venom is like the venom of a snake; they are like the deaf cobra that blocks its ear" — the serpent imagery develops from the falsehood of v. 3 into a specific zoological metaphor. In ANE culture, snake charming was a known practice (Eccl 10:11; Jer 8:17); the "deaf cobra" (pethen ḥērēš) was the species that could not be charmed — notoriously dangerous precisely because it could not be controlled by the charmer's music. The image is of a governing class so thoroughly committed to injustice that even prophetic rebuke has no effect on them — they are immune to the word that would normally call them to account.
5: "That will not listen to the voice of those who charm, no matter how skillfully they do it" — ʾăšer lōʾ-yišmaʿ lĕqôl mĕlaḥăšîm ḥôbēr ḥăbārîm mĕḥukkām. The "charmer" (mĕlaḥaš, whisperer, charmer) uses specific skill to redirect dangerous creatures — in the social world, this figure represents the wise counselor or prophet who uses speech to guide those in power. The reference to skill ("no matter how skillfully") emphasizes that this is not a failure of technique but a resistance to wisdom itself. Jeremiah 8:17 uses the same image for the Babylonian army that cannot be charmed away from judgment: "For I am going to send venomous snakes among you — adders that cannot be charmed, and they will bite you."
6: "Smash their teeth in their mouths, O God; tear out the fangs of the young lions, O LORD!" — the imprecation targets the specific instruments of the wicked's predation: their teeth and fangs. In Psalm 34:10, "young lions (kĕpîrîm)" may go hungry while those who fear YHWH lack nothing — here the imprecation reverses their predatory capacity. Disabling the teeth is disabling the weapon: the judicial violence the wicked administer through their rulings (v. 2) is expressed through the lion-tooth metaphor. The prayer is for the wicked to become what they cannot avoid being without their power — harmless, without the instruments of domination.
7: "Let them drain away like water flowing downhill; when they draw their bows, let their arrows fall harmless" — the sequence of similes in vv. 7–9 piles up images of futility and dissolution. Water flowing away is irreversible — it cannot be gathered back once it runs off. The image of arrows falling harmless mid-flight inverts the military power of the armed warrior: the bow is drawn, the moment of maximum threat, yet the arrow accomplishes nothing. In the Psalter's theology of divine reversal, the powerful are made impotent and the weapons of the wicked misfire in God's presence (Ps 76:3–6).
8: "Let them be like a snail that dissolves as it goes, like a stillborn child that never sees the light of the sun" — the snail (šablûl) was observed in antiquity to leave a trail of slime as it moved, which observers interpreted as the creature dissolving itself away. This is not accurate zoology but is a vivid image of self-consuming dissolution. The nepel (stillborn child, or premature birth that does not survive) was in the ancient world a symbol of complete failure to reach life's potential — conceived but never able to develop (Job 3:16; Eccl 6:3). The comparison positions the wicked as beings who reach neither full life (like the stillborn) nor lasting presence (like the dissolving snail).
9: "Before your cooking pots have felt the heat of the thornwood — both green and dry — he will whirl them away" — one of the most difficult verses in the Psalter; the image is of a fire that is extinguished before it even heats the cooking pots. Thornwood burns hot but briefly — it catches quickly and dies quickly. The "both green and dry" (ḥay wĕḥārôn) may indicate thorns at different stages of readiness, which together still cannot sustain the fire long enough to complete the cooking. The point: the wicked will be swept away before their schemes are completed, before their destructive energy has accomplished anything. The divine judgment is faster than their plans.
10: "The righteous will rejoice when they see justice carried out; they will wash their feet in the blood of the wicked" — this verse is among the most troubling imprecatory images for modern readers. The washing of feet in the blood of enemies echoes the ANE battle context: victorious warriors waded through the blood of the slain (cf. Isa 63:1–6; Rev 14:20; 19:13–15). This is covenant curse imagery — the language of Deuteronomy 28's curses applied to those who have oppressed the innocent. In the ancient world of covenant treaties, curse formulas were graphic and visceral by design — they were meant to convey the severity of the covenant-breaking. The righteous rejoicing (yiśmaḥ ṣaddîq) is the vindication of those who suffered under unjust rulers.
11: "Then people will say, 'There really is a reward for the righteous; truly there is a God who judges the earth'" — the theological conclusion is the payoff of the entire psalm. The skeptics who doubted divine justice (implied throughout) will be silenced by the visible evidence. "A reward for the righteous (pĕrî laṣṣaddîq)" — fruit, concrete result — validates the wisdom tradition's claim that righteous living produces good outcomes under divine governance. "A God who judges (šōpĕṭîm) the earth" — the governing title for the deity who holds final authority over earthly justice. The concluding verse answers the opening question (v. 1: do you rulers decree what is right?) by asserting that if they do not, God does.
